description: The camera is facing a bright window, causing the window to glow white and making the room interior look like a dark silhouette.
action: Shoot from an angle where the window is on your left or right side, not straight ahead. Turn on all interior lights to balance the exposure.
photo_tip: Tap the darkest part of your phone screen to lock focus and raise the exposure before pressing the shutter button.

description: The room has a mix of blue daylight bulbs and yellow warm bulbs on simultaneously, creating muddy, unflattering colors in photos.
action: Replace all bulbs in the listing with warm white (2700K–3000K). Consistency in color temperature is the single biggest lighting upgrade you can make.
photo_tip: Turn off auto-HDR on your phone camera when shooting rooms with mixed light — it makes color conflicts worse, not better.

description: A harsh white reflection from the flash is visible in mirrors, windows, TV screens, or glossy surfaces, ruining the shot.
action: Turn the flash completely off. Turn on every light in the room — overhead, lamps, under-cabinet. Natural + artificial layered light always beats a flash.
photo_tip: Force flash OFF in your camera settings before entering each room. It resets to auto between sessions.

issue: Main Furniture Cropped Out of Frame
description: The bed, sofa, or fireplace — the hero element of the room — is cut off at the frame edge, undermining the photo's impact.
action: Step back further and switch to the 0.5x wide-angle lens on your phone. The full hero piece must be entirely within the frame.
photo_tip: Stand literally in the corner or doorway of the room. Use 0.5x wide angle and shoot at chest height — never above your head.

issue: Dark Corners Making Room Feel Small
description: The corners of the room are hidden in deep shadow, making the space look cramped and gloomy in photos.
action: Place a floor lamp or table lamp in the darkest corner. Even a $25 lamp bouncing light off a white wall dramatically opens up the space.
photo_tip: Shoot during Golden Hour (1 hour after sunrise or before sunset) so natural light reaches deeper into corners.

description: Only one bedside lamp is on, creating an unbalanced, careless look that signals the host doesn't pay attention to detail.
action: Turn on both bedside lamps. Match wattage and bulb color exactly. Symmetry in bedroom photos directly increases perceived listing quality.
photo_tip: Stand at the exact center foot of the bed, hold the phone at chest height perfectly level, and verify both lamps are equal in brightness before shooting.

description: An active TV casts an unnatural blue or flickering glow over the room and pulls the viewer's eye away from the space.
action: Turn the TV off so it's a clean black rectangle, or display a static high-resolution art image on a black background for a styled look.
photo_tip: If the TV off is reflecting the window like a mirror, shift your shooting angle slightly left or right until the reflection disappears.

description: The bathroom is lit by a single dim ceiling bulb, casting shadows over the vanity and making the space look unclean in photos.
action: Turn on every light source in the bathroom — vanity lights, sconces, ceiling fixture. Bright bathrooms signal cleanliness. Replace any burned-out bulbs before the shoot.
photo_tip: Shoot from the doorway using the 0.5x wide-angle lens. Aim slightly toward the vanity mirror to bounce light back into the frame.

description: A bare bulb on a ceiling fan is projecting hard, spider-like shadows across the ceiling and walls, making the room feel chaotic.
action: Turn off the ceiling fan light entirely during the photo shoot. Use floor and table lamps for warmer, more diffused light instead.
photo_tip: Make sure the fan blades are fully stopped before shooting — motion blur on fan blades looks unprofessional even in a still photo.

issue: Exterior Shot Taken in Flat Daylight or Darkness
description: The exterior hero shot is taken at noon under flat grey sky or in complete darkness, looking lifeless and uninviting.
action: Shoot the exterior at Twilight — 10 to 20 minutes after sunset. Turn on every interior and exterior light so the home glows warmly from inside.
photo_tip: Use Night Mode and brace your phone against a wall or use a mini tripod. Hold still for the 3-second exposure to avoid blur.

description: A queen or king bed has one side flush against the wall, creating a cramped, dorm-room look that deters couples and comfort-conscious guests.
action: Center the bed on the main wall with matching nightstands on both sides and minimum 24 inches of walking space on each side.
photo_tip: Shoot straight-on from the foot of the bed at chest height to show the equal spacing on both sides of the bed.

description: A visible mattress without a topper, or a flat synthetic comforter with no texture, signals a cheap stay and photographs terribly.
action: Layer the bed: mattress protector → fitted sheet → flat sheet → duvet with insert in a white duvet cover → 2 sleeping pillows + 2 euro shams + 2 decorative pillows. White hotel bedding is the Airbnb gold standard.
photo_tip: Fluff and karate-chop the pillows right before shooting. Rumpled pillows are the #1 reason bedroom photos look cheap.

description: The bed has no bedside surface, or has one nightstand that is a completely different height and style to the other, looking accidental.
action: Add matching nightstands with lamps on both sides of the bed. They don't need to be expensive — identical IKEA pieces look intentional and professional.
photo_tip: A horizontal (landscape orientation) shot from the foot of the bed best shows the symmetry or lack thereof on both sides.

issue: No Throw or Decorative Layer on Bed
description: The bed is made but has zero texture or decorative layering — it looks flat, clinical, and cold rather than inviting.
action: Add a folded linen throw at the foot of the bed and 2 decorative cushions. Texture = perceived luxury. Cost: under $40.
photo_tip: Shoot from a 45-degree corner angle rather than head-on to show the depth and texture of the layering on the bed.

description: Host's clothing, personal photos, prescription bottles, or toiletries are visible in the bedroom, making guests feel like they're sleeping in someone else's home.
action: Remove every personal item from the bedroom before every shoot and stay. Guests pay for a hotel-like experience, not a house-share. Lock personal items in a dedicated storage space.
photo_tip: Do a 360-degree scan of the room before shooting. Look at every surface, the floor, and inside any open shelving.

description: Thin or sheer curtains let in early morning light, which guests will mention in reviews as a reason they couldn't sleep.
action: Install blackout curtains or a blackout liner behind decorative curtains. This is one of the highest-ROI comfort upgrades for any Airbnb bedroom.
photo_tip: Show curtains fully open and flooded with natural light for the listing photo — but note 'blackout curtains available' in your description.

description: The only closet is full of host belongings, leaving guests with nowhere to hang clothes during a multi-night stay.
action: Clear at least half the closet and add 10 empty hangers. Add a luggage rack so guests never have to put suitcases on the floor or bed.
photo_tip: Open the closet door slightly and show the empty hangers and luggage rack in the photo — it signals thoughtful hosting.

description: A harsh overhead fixture is the only light source, making the bedroom feel like a hospital room rather than a relaxing retreat.
action: Add two bedside table lamps and consider a dimmer on the overhead fixture. Layered, warm light is the #1 mood setter in a bedroom.
photo_tip: For the listing photo, turn off the overhead light and shoot with only the bedside lamps on for a warm, intimate atmosphere.

description: Uncovered windows make a room look unfinished, offer zero privacy, and photograph as harsh glare rectangles.
action: Hang floor-to-ceiling linen or cotton curtain panels. Mount the rod 4–6 inches above the window frame and extend it 6 inches beyond each side to make windows look larger.
photo_tip: For photos, pull curtains fully open so the fabric panels frame the window like a stage curtain, adding width and elegance.

issue: Cheap or Stained Mattress Without Protector
description: A mattress with visible stains, sagging, or no waterproof protector is a hygiene concern that will generate immediate negative reviews.
action: Encase every mattress in a waterproof, noise-free mattress protector before adding sheets. Replace any mattress with permanent staining. Budget $80–$150 for a quality replacement protector.
photo_tip: The mattress under the bedding should never be visible in listing photos — if it is, your bedding layers are insufficient.

description: The bathroom has a plastic soap dispenser, a paper cup holder, and a loose toilet paper roll — the three cheapest signals of a low-budget rental.
action: Buy a 4-piece matching bathroom accessory set (soap dispenser, toothbrush holder, cotton jar, tray) in matte black, chrome, or white. Cost: $25–$45.
photo_tip: Arrange all accessories on a small tray on the counter in a triangle composition: tall item at back, two shorter items in front.

description: Towels in different colors, patterns, or sizes displayed in the bathroom make the space look chaotic and signal low quality.
action: Provide matching sets of white bath towels, hand towels, and washcloths. White signals cleanliness and photographs cleanly. Fold or roll and display them intentionally.
photo_tip: Roll 3 white hand towels and stack them on the counter, or fold a bath towel in thirds draped over a towel bar — both read as luxury in photos.

issue: Visible Mold on Grout or Caulk
description: Black or pink mold on tile grout lines or around the tub caulk is the single fastest way to earn a 1-star hygiene review.
action: Scrub with a bleach-based mold remover. Re-caulk any failed joints with 100% silicone bath caulk. Re-grout discolored lines. This is non-negotiable between every guest stay.
photo_tip: Get close with the camera to shoot the cleaned tile. Clean bright grout lines read as 'spotless' in close-up photos and signal deep cleaning.

issue: Toilet Lid Left Open in Photos
description: An open toilet lid in a listing photo is the #1 most pointed-out detail in Airbnb photo feedback — it looks unsanitary.
action: Close the toilet lid in every single photo. Make it a non-negotiable part of your pre-shoot checklist.
photo_tip: Shoot the bathroom from the doorway to minimize how much of the toilet is visible in the frame. Focus toward the vanity and shower instead.

description: A completely utilitarian bathroom with zero decor — no plant, no art, no candle — reads as cold and uninviting.
action: Add one small potted plant (pothos or succulent), one piece of simple wall art, and a candle or diffuser on the counter. The bathroom is a spa experience, not a gas station restroom.
photo_tip: A green plant in the corner of a bathroom photo adds an organic contrast that makes the space feel alive — even in a tiny space.

issue: Shower Curtain is Plastic or Stained
description: A cheap clear plastic or visibly stained shower curtain makes the entire bathroom look dirty regardless of how clean it is.
action: Replace with a white fabric shower curtain and matching rings in chrome or matte black. Replace the liner (the plastic inner waterproof layer) between every guest stay.
photo_tip: Pull the shower curtain fully closed so it hangs straight and shows no gaps. Straight, clean curtain lines read as orderly and clean.

description: Guests arriving to a bathroom with only a partial roll and no backup feel immediately anxious — this appears in reviews as 'host doesn't think about guests'.
action: Stock 2 full backup rolls visibly (on a holder, on the tank, or in a basket). Display them intentionally, not stuffed under the sink.
photo_tip: A small wicker basket with 2–3 rolled spare toilet paper rolls on the bathroom floor is a tiny detail that photographs beautifully.

issue: Hair Visible in Shower or on Surfaces
description: Any visible hair — in the drain, on the floor, or on surfaces — is an immediate hygiene failure that guarantees a negative review.
action: Add a drain hair catcher in every shower and clean it after every guest. Add a lint roller to your cleaning kit for surfaces. Do a final white-glove check on all bathroom surfaces.
photo_tip: Use your phone flashlight on bathroom floors and shower walls before shooting — it reveals hair that normal lighting hides.

description: A spotty or streaky bathroom mirror photographs as a dirty, neglected space even when the rest of the bathroom is clean.
action: Clean all mirrors with a microfiber cloth and glass cleaner immediately before every shoot and every guest arrival. A clean mirror is the most photographed element in a bathroom.
photo_tip: Stand to the side of the mirror, not directly in front, so you avoid capturing your own reflection in the shot.

issue: Caulk Cracking Around Tub or Shower
description: Failed caulk at the tub-wall junction or shower base lets water penetrate behind the tile, causing mold and rot that guests can see and smell.
action: Remove all old caulk with a caulk remover tool. Let dry 24 hours. Apply fresh 100% silicone caulk. Smooth with a wet finger. This repair costs $8 and prevents hundreds in water damage.
photo_tip: The caulk lines around the tub are clearly visible in wide bathroom shots. Fresh white caulk lines read as 'recently renovated' to guests browsing listings.

description: Every piece of furniture is pushed to the perimeter, creating an awkward empty center and a waiting-room atmosphere.
action: Float the sofa 12–18 inches from the wall. Create a conversation cluster with chairs facing each other anchored by a coffee table. The room instantly feels more intentional.
photo_tip: Shoot from a corner of the room at 45 degrees to show the depth of the conversation arrangement — straight-on shots flatten the layout.

issue: Rug Too Small for Seating Group
description: A tiny rug sits alone in the middle of the floor without touching any furniture, looking like a forgotten doormat.
action: Use a rug large enough for the front legs of every sofa and chair to rest on it. Minimum 8x10 feet for most living rooms. A properly sized rug anchors the entire space.
photo_tip: Aim the camera slightly downward when shooting the living room to include the rug and show how it relates to the furniture legs.

description: The coffee table is covered in remotes, magazines, charging cables, and random objects — the most photographed surface in any living room.
action: Style the coffee table with the rule of three: one tray, one small plant or candle, one coffee table book. Keep the TV remote in the tray. Nothing else.
photo_tip: The coffee table is almost always in the foreground of living room photos. Style it perfectly — it's the first thing guests' eyes land on.

issue: No Throw Pillows or Blanket on Sofa
description: A bare sofa with no cushions or textile layers looks cold, sparse, and signals the host hasn't invested in guest comfort.
action: Add 4 coordinated throw pillows in 2 complementary colors/textures and one folded throw blanket draped over the sofa arm. Minimal investment, massive photographic impact.
photo_tip: Arrange pillows asymmetrically — two leaning upright at the back, one smaller in front at an angle. Rigidly symmetrical pillows look staged and fake.

description: The TV is mounted near the ceiling, requiring guests to crane their necks upward — a discomfort that generates negative reviews.
action: Mount the TV so the center of the screen is at seated eye level: 42–48 inches from floor to screen center. Lower is almost always better.
photo_tip: In living room photos, a too-high TV creates a visually awkward composition where it dominates the upper frame. A properly mounted TV sits naturally in the frame.

description: Power strips on the floor, TV cables dangling down the wall, and exposed extension cords make any space look messy and cheap.
action: Run TV cables through a paintable cord cover raceway on the wall ($12). Hide power strips behind furniture. Use velcro cable ties on any bundled cords.
photo_tip: Before every listing photo shoot, do a dedicated cord audit from floor level — you'll be amazed what you miss from standing height.

issue: No Focal Point or Statement Wall
description: The living room has no visual anchor — no art, no accent wall, no interesting feature — making it forgettable and hard to differentiate in search results.
action: Create a focal point: hang one large-format print (minimum 24x36 inches) above the sofa, or style a gallery wall with 5–7 coordinated frames. Art is the cheapest high-impact upgrade.
photo_tip: Frame your hero living room shot so the artwork or focal wall is always visible. Guests decide to click on a listing within 2 seconds — your focal point needs to be in frame.

description: Built-in shelves or bookcases are either completely empty or randomly stacked with books and objects, looking like a storage unit.
action: Style shelves using the bookend method: stack some books horizontally, lean a framed print, add one plant and one decorative object per shelf. Mix books with non-book objects.
photo_tip: A styled bookcase in the background of a living room photo adds depth, character, and the impression that the space is thoughtfully curated.

description: Zero greenery or organic elements makes any space feel artificial, sterile, and photogenically flat.
action: Add at least one medium-to-large plant (pothos, snake plant, fiddle-leaf fig) in a stylish pot. Plants increase perceived value and photograph beautifully against neutral walls.
photo_tip: Position a tall plant in a corner visible from the main shooting angle — it fills dead space, adds color contrast, and draws the eye into the room.

description: Closed, broken, or yellowed window blinds block the most powerful free design element — natural light — and make spaces look gloomy and neglected.
action: Open all blinds and curtains fully for photo shoots. Replace any broken or yellowed blinds. Consider replacing horizontal blinds with white roller shades or linen curtain panels.
photo_tip: Never close the blinds in listing photos. Natural light is your most powerful photography tool — it makes spaces feel larger and cleaner than any editing can.

description: Kitchen counters covered in small appliances, mail, fruit bowls, and utensil crocks make the kitchen look cramped and hard to use.
action: Apply the Airbnb counter rule: only 3 items on the counter are allowed in photos — coffee maker, one decorative item, and one plant or fruit bowl. Store everything else.
photo_tip: Shoot from a low angle (counter height) to exaggerate the depth of the counter and make it look longer and more expansive.

description: A stovetop with grease splatter, burnt residue, or stained grates is the fastest route to a hygiene complaint and a 1-star cleanliness review.
action: Deep clean the stovetop before every guest arrival and listing shoot. For stubborn grease, use a degreaser and razor blade scraper on glass tops. Replace visibly worn grates.
photo_tip: Shoot the kitchen from an angle that shows the stovetop clearly — if it's clean, it builds trust. Never hide it. Guests will check it the moment they arrive.

issue: Mismatched or Expired Pantry Items Visible
description: Half-empty condiment bottles, mismatched Tupperware lids, and expired spices left by previous guests are a hygiene and hospitality failure.
action: Provide a fresh, organized starter kit: olive oil, salt, pepper, coffee, and tea at minimum. Remove all previous guest food. Label shelves clearly for guest use vs. host storage.
photo_tip: Leave the pantry or a kitchen cabinet slightly open in one shot to show organized, guest-ready supplies — it's a hospitality trust signal.

description: Guests booking an Airbnb specifically to avoid hotels still expect a morning coffee setup. A missing coffee maker is a consistently cited complaint.
action: Provide at minimum a drip coffee maker, kettle, coffee, tea, sugar, and creamer. A Nespresso machine elevates perceived value disproportionately to its cost ($80–$150).
photo_tip: Style a dedicated coffee station with the machine, mugs stacked beside it, and a small plant or candle. It photographs as a premium hospitality detail.

description: A kitchen sink that drains slowly or emits odor makes guests feel the kitchen is unsanitary regardless of how it looks.
action: Run boiling water with baking soda and vinegar down the drain weekly. Clean the garbage disposal with ice cubes and citrus peels. Use a mesh drain strainer to prevent future clogs.
photo_tip: Run the faucet briefly and photograph the sink area with clean, dry surfaces — an empty, spotless sink reads as a freshly cleaned kitchen.

description: Guests who book a place with a kitchen expect to cook. One frying pan and two mismatched plates signals an afterthought, not a functional kitchen.
action: Stock: 2 pots, 2 pans (one non-stick), baking sheet, cutting board, knife set, full utensil set, and place settings for the maximum guest count + 2 extra. Label what's available in the welcome guide.
photo_tip: A beautifully organized open shelf showing stacked white plates, matching glasses, and neat cookware communicates a 'fully equipped kitchen' without a word of text.

description: A fridge with spills, old food smells, or mystery stains from previous guests is an immediate 1-star review trigger.
action: Deep clean the interior before every guest with baking soda solution. Leave an open box of baking soda on a shelf to neutralize odors between visits. Check the fridge is set between 35–38°F.
photo_tip: Open the fridge and photograph the interior — a clean, organized fridge with a few stocked essentials (water, condiments, creamer) reads as premium hosting.

description: A splattered, stained microwave interior is consistently ranked as one of the top hygiene complaints in short-term rental reviews.
action: Microwave a bowl of water with lemon juice for 5 minutes before every guest — the steam loosens splatter so it wipes clean in 30 seconds. Check the turntable is seated correctly.
photo_tip: Open the microwave door slightly in kitchen shots — a clean interior visible through the door is a powerful hygiene trust signal.

issue: No Dish Soap, Sponge, or Cleaning Supplies
description: Guests staying more than one night need to wash dishes. Missing cleaning supplies forces guests to leave dishes dirty or buy their own supplies.
action: Stock: dish soap, a new sponge, dishwasher pods (if applicable), trash bags in the correct sizes, and a surface cleaner spray under the kitchen sink. Replenish before every guest.
photo_tip: A neatly organized under-sink cabinet with clearly visible supplies — labeled if possible — shows in photos as a host who thinks of everything.

description: A full or odorous trash can under the sink or in the kitchen signals poor turnover hygiene and sets a bad first impression the moment guests arrive.
action: Empty every trash bin in the property before every guest arrival. Replace with a fresh bag. Use a trash can with a lid for the kitchen. Add a small can with lid in every bedroom and bathroom.
photo_tip: In kitchen photos, ensure the trash can is either completely hidden or has a closed lid. An open or visible trash can in a listing photo is an immediate listing quality downgrade.

description: Peeling or faded exterior paint is the single biggest factor in a low first-impression score for arriving guests and external listing photos.
action: Repaint the front-facing facade and trim. Use a premium exterior latex paint. Even a fresh coat of the same color reads as 'well-maintained' versus 'neglected'.
photo_tip: Shoot the exterior from a 45-degree angle rather than straight-on — it shows depth and architectural dimension that flat-front shots miss.

description: Untrimmed shrubs, patchy lawn, and dead plants in the front yard make guests feel their booking was a mistake before they even open the door.
action: Mow, edge, and trim before every listing photo and periodically during high-season bookings. Add fresh dark mulch to all beds. One flowering plant at the entry dramatically lifts curb appeal.
photo_tip: Shoot the exterior on a clear morning when the lawn is freshly cut. Dewey grass and sharp shadows on a sunny morning make properties look like magazine shoots.

description: A bare porch or entry with no plants, no mat, and no lighting reads as uninviting — guests have no visual cue that this is a warm, welcoming space.
action: Add: two matching potted plants flanking the door, a quality non-slip doormat, and a porch light that is always on at arrival time. Total cost under $80.
photo_tip: Include the front door and potted plants in the same frame. The plants frame the door like a natural welcome arch and anchor the composition.

issue: Dumpsters or Trash Cans Visible in Photos
description: A listing hero photo showing trash cans, recycling bins, or a dumpster makes an otherwise attractive property look like a rental complex.
action: Move all trash receptacles out of frame before every exterior photo. Permanently store them behind a fence, gate, or shed if possible.
photo_tip: Walk the perimeter before shooting. Look for bins, garden hoses, garden tools, or utility items that sneak into the frame.

issue: Cracked or Sunken Driveway or Walkway
description: A cracked, sunken, or weed-filled walkway creates a safety hazard and makes guests feel the property is poorly maintained.
action: Fill cracks with concrete caulk. Pull all weeds from expansion joints. A bag of crack filler and 30 minutes transforms the guest's first-impression walkway.
photo_tip: Shoot the walkway from ground level looking toward the front door — it creates a welcoming perspective shot that draws viewers into the listing.

issue: No Outdoor Seating or Lifestyle Area
description: A patio, balcony, or yard with no furniture is a missed amenity that could justify higher nightly rates and differentiate the listing.
action: Add at minimum two outdoor chairs and a small table. A bistro set costs under $100 and photographs as a premium amenity. Add a string of outdoor lights for evening ambiance.
photo_tip: Shoot the outdoor seating area at twilight with the string lights on. This single photo justifies premium pricing and creates strong booking intent.

description: Guests arriving at night cannot find the property because house numbers are missing, rusted, or blocked by foliage.
action: Install large, illuminated house numbers (minimum 4 inches tall) clearly visible from the street. Trim any foliage blocking them. Include a pin-accurate map link in the welcome message.
photo_tip: Include the house number in at least one exterior shot — it builds trust by confirming the address matches the listing.

description: A scuffed, faded, or visually dated front door is the handshake of the property and a missed opportunity to create a powerful first impression.
action: Repaint or replace the front door. A bold color — matte black, navy, deep green — consistently receives higher aesthetic ratings in listing reviews. Add new matching hardware.
photo_tip: A closeup shot of a beautiful front door with the right paint color, clean hardware, and potted plant beside it performs extremely well as a secondary listing photo.

description: Guests arriving after dark to a property with no pathway or porch lighting feel unsafe and underserved before they've even stepped inside.
action: Install a porch light on a dusk-to-dawn sensor or smart timer. Add solar pathway lights along the walkway. Budget $40–$80 for a complete exterior lighting upgrade.
photo_tip: Photograph the exterior at dusk with all exterior lights on and interior lights glowing warmly — this creates the most emotionally compelling listing photo possible.

issue: Pool or Hot Tub Not Staged
description: A pool or hot tub shown in listing photos without staging — no towels, no floating accessories, no lighting — looks like a maintenance photo, not an amenity photo.
action: Stage the pool/hot tub area with folded towels on lounge chairs, a drinks tray, and floating accessories. Turn on any underwater lighting. These photos justify the highest nightly premiums.
photo_tip: Shoot the pool at twilight from a low angle near the water surface. The underwater lights and reflections are dramatically more compelling than a midday overhead shot.

issue: No Smoke Detector in Sleeping Area
description: Airbnb requires a working smoke detector in or near every sleeping area. Absence is a policy violation, a legal liability, and a life-safety emergency.
action: Install a combination smoke/CO detector in every bedroom and in the hallway outside sleeping areas. Test monthly. Replace units over 10 years old. This is non-negotiable.
photo_tip: Photograph each smoke detector on the ceiling clearly labeled with its test date. Include in your listing's safety section to demonstrate compliance to guests.

description: Any property with a gas appliance, furnace, or attached garage must have CO detectors. CO is odorless and kills silently — and Airbnb mandates them.
action: Install a CO detector on every level with sleeping areas and within 15 feet of any gas appliance. Use combination smoke/CO units. Test monthly and log the date.
photo_tip: Show the CO detector in a hallway photo near the sleeping area — guests actively look for this now and it increases booking confidence.

issue: No Fire Extinguisher in Kitchen Area
description: A kitchen without a readily accessible fire extinguisher is both an Airbnb policy gap and a basic safety failure for any cooking-enabled space.
action: Mount an ABC-rated 2.5 lb fire extinguisher within reach of the kitchen, at chest height, clearly visible. Check the pressure gauge monthly. Show it in your welcome guide.
photo_tip: Mount the extinguisher in a highly visible location and include it in a kitchen overview shot. Guests booking with families specifically look for this.

description: Stairs with 4 or more risers require a secure, graspable handrail by code. A missing or wobbly railing is a fall injury liability and automatic listing flag.
action: Install a code-compliant handrail (34–38 inches above the nose of the stair) secured into wall studs. Test by applying lateral force — it must not move. Inspect before every guest stay.
photo_tip: Always include the staircase in listing photos if it's a feature. A clean, secure handrail with good lighting makes the staircase a selling point rather than a concern.

description: Airbnb's hosting standards recommend a first aid kit. Absence signals a host who has not thought about guest wellbeing.
action: Provide a clearly labeled basic first aid kit (bandages, antiseptic wipes, pain reliever, antihistamine) in an accessible, visible location. Note its location in the welcome guide.
photo_tip: Mount a clearly labeled white first aid kit on a wall in the kitchen or bathroom and include it in your safety amenities photos.

issue: Lock on Bedroom Door Missing or Broken
description: Guests sharing a property with other renters or the host expect a functioning lock on bedroom doors for privacy and security.
action: Install a privacy lock (passage lock with inside privacy lever) on every bedroom door. For shared spaces, consider a smart lock on the bedroom for extra security.
photo_tip: Show the bedroom door handle and lock clearly in a detail shot — for shared hosting situations, this is a decisive booking trust signal.

issue: Pool Has No Safety Barrier or Gate
description: An unfenced pool at a property where children may stay is a drowning risk, a legal liability, and may violate local ordinances.
action: Install a self-closing, self-latching pool fence at minimum 4 feet tall. This is required by law in most jurisdictions and by Airbnb's hosting standards for family listings.
photo_tip: Show the pool fence and gate in your listing photos — for family bookings, this is a positive trust signal, not a detractor.

description: Guests in an unfamiliar property have no idea how to exit quickly in an emergency or where to go if they need help.
action: Post a laminated emergency card in every bedroom and near the front door with: emergency exit route, local emergency number, nearest hospital address, and host phone number.
photo_tip: A neatly framed emergency information card on a wall adds a professional, hotel-like touch that photographs as attentive, responsible hosting.

issue: Slippery Bathroom Floor with No Non-Slip Mat
description: A wet bathroom floor with no mat or non-slip surface is the #1 cause of guest injuries in short-term rentals.
action: Place a non-slip bath mat outside the shower/tub and install non-slip strips inside the tub/shower floor. Wash the bath mat between every guest stay.
photo_tip: A freshly washed, fluffy bath mat on the floor frames the bathroom entrance and adds a plush, welcoming texture to bathroom photos.

issue: GFCI Outlets Missing in Bathroom or Kitchen
description: Outlets within 6 feet of a water source without GFCI protection are an electrocution hazard and a code violation in all jurisdictions.
action: Replace all outlets near sinks and water sources with GFCI outlets. This is a $15 part and 15-minute DIY fix per outlet. Test the GFCI button monthly.
photo_tip: Outlet safety isn't photogenic, but a caption noting 'all outlets GFCI-protected' in your listing description builds strong trust with safety-conscious guests.

issue: No Welcome Book or House Manual
description: Guests arriving without instructions for WiFi, appliances, check-out, or local recommendations feel lost and are more likely to message with basic questions — and leave lower scores.
action: Create a physical or digital welcome book covering: WiFi password, appliance instructions, trash day, check-out procedure, and 5–10 local restaurant recommendations. A Canva template takes 30 minutes.
photo_tip: Place the welcome book on the coffee table or kitchen counter and include it in a lifestyle shot — it signals a thoughtful, well-organized host.

description: Guests' first action upon arrival is connecting to WiFi. A password that's hard to find or missing triggers an immediate frustration that colors the entire stay.
action: Frame the WiFi name and password in a small, attractive print on a kitchen counter or near the TV. Use a QR code for easy connection. Update the framed card whenever the password changes.
photo_tip: A beautifully framed WiFi password sign on a kitchen counter or desk is one of the most noticed and appreciated details in listing photos.

issue: No Iron or Hair Dryer Provided
description: Business travelers and guests attending events specifically filter for these amenities. Missing them causes immediate listing filter-outs and negative reviews.
action: Provide a functioning iron with an ironing board (or hanging steamer) and a wall-mounted hair dryer in every bathroom. These are expected, not premium, in any listing over $80/night.
photo_tip: Mount the hair dryer with a holder on the inside of a cabinet door or on the bathroom wall — it keeps it tidy and demonstrates a thought-through bathroom setup.

issue: No Blackout Option for Early Sleepers
description: Guests in summer destinations or east-facing rooms who face 5am sunrise light through thin curtains reliably cite it as a negative experience.
action: Install blackout curtains or a blackout roller blind in every bedroom. This is the single most frequently cited comfort upgrade in short-term rental host communities.
photo_tip: Photograph the bedroom with the blackout curtains drawn partially closed so their thickness and coverage is visible — it's a selling feature, especially for late-sleepers.

issue: No Extra Blanket or Pillow Accessible
description: Guests who are too cold at night but cannot find extra bedding will mention it in reviews. It's a tiny fix with outsized impact on comfort scores.
action: Leave a clearly visible extra blanket (folded in a basket or on a shelf) in every bedroom. Note its location in the welcome guide. Add spare pillows in the closet.
photo_tip: A woven basket with a folded throw blanket in the corner of a bedroom adds texture to photos and communicates comfort and abundance.

description: Guests spending more than one night hate putting their suitcase on the floor or the bed. A missing luggage rack is a small absence that reads as an oversight.
action: Add a folding luggage rack in every bedroom. Keep it folded open and visible in the corner near the closet. Cost: $25–$40.
photo_tip: A folding luggage rack in the corner of a bedroom photo communicates that the host thinks about functional guest needs — not just aesthetics.

issue: No Streaming Service or Smart TV
description: Guests under 40 do not watch cable. A TV with only cable or no apps means they can't watch Netflix, and this is cited in reviews far more often than hosts expect.
action: Ensure the TV has Netflix, YouTube, and at least one other streaming app. Provide a Firestick or Apple TV if the TV is not smart. Leave login instructions in the welcome book.
photo_tip: Show the TV screen displaying a streaming service home screen in your living area photo — it confirms smart TV functionality without any text description needed.

description: WiFi quality is now the #1 amenity expectation for Airbnb guests. A connection below 25 Mbps or with dead zones generates the most booking-killing negative reviews.
action: Upgrade to a plan with minimum 100 Mbps. Add a mesh WiFi extender (Eero, Google Nest) to eliminate dead zones. Display the Ookla speed test result in the welcome book as a trust signal.
photo_tip: A screenshot of your speed test result (100+ Mbps) embedded in your listing description or host-highlight photos is a powerful differentiator in urban markets.

issue: No Check-In Instructions or Lockbox Access
description: Guests arriving after hours or at an unclear address with no self-check-in option have a stressful arrival that they associate with the listing, not external circumstances.
action: Install a smart lock or combination lockbox on the front door. Send detailed arrival instructions (address pin, parking, lock code, door direction) 24 hours before check-in.
photo_tip: A photo of the front door with the lockbox or smart lock visible is a decisive trust element for guests choosing between listings — it eliminates arrival anxiety.

description: The fastest-growing Airbnb guest segment is remote workers booking 7+ night stays. A listing with no desk or chair loses this entire segment.
action: Designate a workspace: a desk, an ergonomic chair, a power outlet, and good lamp. If space is limited, a counter stool at the kitchen island with a clear surface qualifies. Tag 'laptop-friendly workspace' in amenities.
photo_tip: A styled desk with a lamp, notebook, small plant, and visible power outlet is one of the highest-performing photo subjects for attracting remote worker bookings.
